author: han0110
content:
It’s mainly because previously we don’t have the program vk released, which requires prover to generate, so we use the same ere-server to serve the verifier.

But now we have program vk released so the verifier mode in zkboost makes sense (I also have an unfinished branch for the same thing locally, but stuck at some point because some verifiers can’t be built together)

Will review the PR soon (did a quick check and it looks nice, thanks!)

For ZisK they provide simple verifier crate so I believe it’s doable, but haven’t checked for other zkVMs (some have their prover together in the same crate, not sure if it’s easy to do such build), will try to dig deeper these days.

author: jsign
content:
Yes, this is an important point that needs to be properly understood -- sharing here a bit my thoughts.

Regarding generating proofs, I suspect, as Han said, that most of the proofs will be created without ere by sophisticated builders/provers. Simply because they will keep optimizing forever the proving process since it is costly. And maybe these tools won't be OSS. 
But even for the proving case,  for me is very important that there is something open source that is as easy as possible and well-documented to generate proofs for the rest of people. Although for proving we need 1 out of N assumption, we need the "1'" to be doable by a mortal.  (This wasn't part of the point above, but I like to highlight this too). 

Reg verification which was the point above, I agree that is prob the most important part for most of the network, and I see it in "stages":
- Short term, we are using zkboost mainly as a way to facilitate infrastructure given that zkVM teams don't have verification libraries for all languages... so integration can be quite hard. I think we are using this now mainly to not be blocked by this current reality.
- The next stage would be CLs using zkVM verification libraries directly inside their software... being because zkVMs have libs for each language, or all languages call C-like libraries build in whatever way. and ofc audited.
- And the ultimate (and best) level, is not relying on zkVM teams software, and having clear specs on how the verifier should work so can be independently implemented. Cody from our team has been exploring this by trying to come up with python specs for openvm and zisk, which has allowed to get closer to an independently built verifier for both ([openvm](https://codygunton.github.io/openvm/_modules/protocol/stark.html#verify_stark) and [zisk](https://codygunton.github.io/pil2-proofman/_modules/protocol/verifier.html#stark_verify) examples). This would remove trust from zkVM teams.

author: han0110
content:
Sorry for all the confusion 🙏

Ere provides 3 things:
1. Prover Rust library to do prove
2. Verifier Rust library to do verify
3. `ere-server` as a docker image wrapping 1 and 2 as http endpoints.

Currently zkboost uses 3 to do verification, and PR 61 is integrating 2 into zkboost directly.

author: jsign
content: yes, but the point before was about gpu needs. i agree the GiBs need can be annoying and not sure how much can be compessed

author: han0110
content:
Currently in `ethereum-package` if we don't specify gpu `count` or `device_ids`, it'll spawn `ere-server` without needing gpu, which runs a more lightweight image (without cuda runtime)

(with cuda the image will have `-cuda` suffix like `ghcr.io/eth-act/ere/ere-server-zisk:0.8.1-cuda`, without it'd be like `ghcr.io/eth-act/ere/ere-server-zisk:0.8.1`)

author: codygunton
content: OK, so, the point being made is that we might want a diversity of implementations of verifiers. We've spoken a lot about having a diversity of provers, but implicitly we've always planned to that each zkvm vendor would ship a single verifier for its prover. <@412614104222531604> do you find this problematic, even in the situation where we have some k of n setup with n >= 3? How much of your (and others') concern is just that the canonical verifiers are all packaged together by ere?

author: codygunton
content: Regarding speccing, after conversations with several people the conclusion was that it's acceptable for the purposes of CL testing to just FFI to the prod verifiers and consider the verifier specs out of scope for the CL specs, the argument being that nobody is going to implement a verifier from the CL specs. Independently, we (testers, auditors, fv engineers, and others) need to understand the proving systems, so I made python implementations. If there is a need for python specs, those could be used. I am in favor of a paradigm where the zkvm teams maintain executable specs, in fact I'd prefer these over white papers.

author: codygunton
content: Another dimension to this is Lean. The library ArkLib and its friend CompPoly are being developed so that we can write specs in Lean and then actually have machine proofs that the protocols are secure. Hax is being developed to extract the Rust verifiers to Lean so that we can have a machine proof that the Rust code actually matches the Lean spec. Proof != perfect assurance, but the proofs are meaningful, they provide precise quantification of our assumptions, and maintaining the fv invariant is a stronger form of updating an uncoupled literate spec, or a loosely coupled but testable python spec. I think Lean stuff is not all that far off, and actually this weekend in Rome I'll attend a day-long conference on ArkLib where I hope to get a better sense of the development timeline. I think there's a reasonable chance this is all ready in time for optional proofs, but also I feel intermediate steps are reasonable.

author: codygunton
content: Final point on portability--afaik Ethproofs continues to require the zkvms to ship WASM verifiers. Idk if that's much of an improvement relative to rust verifiers, maybe?
